message.content: Adversaries may achieve persistence on macOS systems by modifying Mach-O binary headers to include a malicious LC_LOAD_DYLIB command. This technique involves altering the binary to load and execute a malicious dynamic library (dylib) whenever the binary runs. The LC_LOAD_DYLIB header specifies which dylibs to load during execution, and adversaries can add this header to a compiled binary using available tools. Although such modifications invalidate the binary's digital signature, adversaries can bypass this by removing the LC_CODE_SIGNATURE command, preventing signature checks at load time. The impact of this technique includes persistent execution of malicious code, potentially leading to unauthorized access and control over the system. Common exploitation methods involve tampering with legitimate binaries to include malicious dylibs. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and validating the integrity of binaries, employing strict code-signing policies, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect anomalous binary modifications. Regular system audits and employing application whitelisting can also help in identifying and preventing such persistence mechanisms.
